risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomisation was conducted using pre-generated random permuted blocks (block sizes of four and six). A data manager at the York Trials Unit, who was completely independent of the research team, created the randomisation programme, which was used to allocate participants to one of the two treatment arms" Comment: This describes a proper random allocation sequence using pre-generated random permuted blocks, which is a truly random method and is sufficient for Low risk.

Quote: "After completing the baseline assessment, the research nurse telephoned a secure and remote randomisation service, located at the York Trials Unit (University of York, UK)." Comment: Allocation was concealed by a central, remote randomisation service accessed by telephone, which prevents the enrolling nurse from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "staff and participants could not be blinded to treatment" and "the nurse treating the patient and also the patient" determined treatment-related decisions. Comment: The study explicitly states that participants and staff were not blinded, and the interventions (NPWT device versus standard care dressings) were obviously different. Outcomes such as healing time, pain, treatment changes, and adverse events could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, so the dominant risk for this domain is high.

Quote: "A blinded outcome assessor (experienced Tissue Viability nurse not involved in the day-to-day conduct of the trial) then assessed photographs for (1) assessment of healing..." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly blinded to allocation and was independent of trial conduct. The main healing outcome was assessed from digital photographs rather than by the treating clinicians, which reduces the chance that knowledge of treatment could influence the judgment of wound healing. Although healing assessment is somewhat subjective, the study piloted a blinded photographic assessment process, so detection bias for this outcome is unlikely.

Quote: "There was no attrition recorded during this trial. From the 12 recruited participants, 5 (2 NPWT and 3 SC participants) exited the trial after completing the maximum 6-month follow-up. A further five participants (two NPWT and three SC) completed less than the 6-month follow-up as they were recruited later into the study. Two participants died during the trial; the official causes of their deaths were not recorded for the purposes of this study; however from the serious adverse event forms, the nurse reported that both participants had infections (chest infection and urinary tract infection) and their health was reported as generally deteriorating prior to death." Comment: Follow-up was essentially complete for the randomized participants, and the shorter follow-up in some participants was due to later recruitment rather than loss to follow-up. The paper reports no attrition and does not suggest differential missing outcome data by group that would plausibly bias the results.
